summary: My work with the Seattle Symphony came to a complete halt on March 11, 2020, when management sent us home during rehearsal. Since then, we've been active on social media, producing short videos and recordings to support the Symphony, including a duet with Jeffrey Barker for fundraising. We've also adapted by creating virtual concerts and educational content, like tiny tots concerts and flute section recordings. Despite the pause in live performances, the Symphony has continued to pay us through mid-April and secured payroll protection funds to maintain base salaries.

Jeffrey Barker and I have been collaborating remotely during the quarantine; we recorded a duet that was used as part of a fundraising drive for the Seattle Symphony. We also teamed up to conduct weekly master classes for the Seattle Youth Symphony flute section over Zoom, working with students on major flute excerpts. Jeffrey has been a great partner in adapting our teaching and performance to the online format during these challenging times.
cues: Seattle Youth Symphony flute section master classes

summary: Early in the quarantine, I recorded a part for the National Flute Association's project called The Prayer Project, which featured ninety-four flutists from seventeen countries. It was exciting to see the final video come together with JoAnn Falletta conducting, showcasing a beautifully edited performance that went beyond just blocks of players. Participating in this global collaboration was a unique experience during a time when live music was halted.

I was involved in a project that Amanda Harberg was part of, which was conducted by JoAnn Falletta and featured in the National Flute Association's The Prayer Project. It was wonderful to see Amanda's involvement in such a large-scale virtual collaboration during the pandemic. The project highlighted how musicians worldwide could come together despite being physically apart.

JoAnn Falletta conducted The Prayer Project, a virtual piece involving ninety-four flutists from around the world, including myself. It was thrilling to see her leadership bring this complex online collaboration to life during the pandemic. The final video was thoughtfully edited, alternating between players rather than just showing blocks of musicians, which made it very engaging.

The Prayer Project was a special virtual piece I recorded for early in the quarantine, involving ninety-four flutists from seventeen countries. Conducted by JoAnn Falletta and organized by the National Flute Association, it was a significant collaborative effort during a time when live performances were impossible. Seeing the final product was rewarding, as it creatively showcased musicians from around the globe.

Nicola Mazzanti, a piccolo player based in Florence, Italy, started hosting master classes on Facebook livestream during the pandemic. He invited several colleagues worldwide to join, and I participated in one of these sessions a few weeks ago. It was an interesting and different experience from my usual work, expanding my reach and connecting me with international musicians.

Florence, Italy, is where Nicola Mazzanti, a piccolo player I know, is based. During the quarantine, he began hosting master classes via Facebook livestream from there, inviting musicians worldwide to participate. This city became a hub for virtual flute education and collaboration during the pandemic.

One of my private students moved back to Korea during the quarantine, so I now teach them via Skype since they don't have the right technology for Zoom. Despite the distance, I continue to maintain lessons with students in Korea and elsewhere, adapting to the challenges of remote teaching. This has been part of how I've kept my private studio active during the pandemic.

The Pacific Northwest Ballet orchestra, like the Seattle Symphony, has been heavily affected by the pandemic, with no clear return date. While they are receiving some pay, many musicians in that orchestra and others are struggling financially. I feel fortunate that the Seattle Symphony has been able to maintain salaries through payroll protection, but I know the ballet orchestra faces uncertainty.

During the quarantine, I spent a lot of time researching the current COVID-19 numbers in King County to stay informed and safe. This local data has been important for planning my activities, like outdoor teaching and walks. King County's situation has influenced how I manage my work and personal life during the pandemic.

The University of Washington Wind Ensemble has been hosting chats with professional musicians and composers during the pandemic. Professor Tim Salzman often asks guests about the future of music, which has sparked thoughtful discussions. These sessions have been a way to stay connected to the academic music community despite the disruptions caused by COVID-19.

Tim Salzman, a professor involved with the University of Washington Wind Ensemble, regularly hosts conversations with guest musicians and composers. He asks insightful questions about the future of music, encouraging reflection on how the pandemic might shape the industry. His leadership has helped maintain engagement and dialogue in the music community during these uncertain times.

John DiCesare, a tuba player and former band director, was a guest in one of the University of Washington Wind Ensemble's virtual sessions. He shared his optimistic view that music will survive the pandemic, noting that if symphony work ends, he can still teach music. John has been very active in online music projects and is a standout in our digital presence during this time.

When thinking about the current pandemic, I often reflect on the 1918 flu pandemic, which also caused terrible shutdowns. History shows that eventually things got back to normal, and I believe the same will happen with music and live performances. This perspective gives me hope that the long-term future of music will be fine despite the current difficulties.

Dennis Bouriakov has been putting out some amazing online content during the quarantine, including his performance of the Prokofiev Sonata. I've been meaning to watch more of his work and learn from it, as it's been a great resource while live concerts are on hold. His recordings have been a bright spot in the virtual music scene for me.

The Prokofiev Sonata, performed by Dennis Bouriakov, is a piece I've admired online during the quarantine. Watching his rendition has been inspiring and a reminder of the high level of artistry still accessible despite the lack of live concerts. It's one of the many virtual performances that have helped me stay connected to music during this time.

My daughter, her husband, and my granddaughter live in Boston, and we FaceTime about four or five times a week. During these calls, I often read classic children's books like Little Red Riding Hood and Goldilocks and the Three Bears to my granddaughter Mimi. Staying connected with my family in Boston has been a comforting part of my routine during the pandemic.

I bought a copy of Little Red Riding Hood to read to my granddaughter during our FaceTime storytimes. Along with other classic children's books, it helps keep us connected and provides a fun way to spend time together despite the distance. These story sessions have become a cherished part of my week.

Goldilocks and the Three Bears is another classic storybook I purchased to share with my granddaughter during our frequent FaceTime calls. Reading these familiar tales helps bridge the distance between us and brings joy to our interactions. It's been a special way to maintain family bonds during the pandemic.
